title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- History and Physical
content: Absence of tonsil
query_used:
A 3-month-old boy presents to his pediatrician with persistent diarrhea, oral candidiasis, and signs and symptoms suggestive of respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) pneumonia. His weight is in the 10th percentile. He is being evaluated for an immunodeficiency disease. Laboratory results for the HIV are negative by PCR. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings in this patient?

Options:
A: Defective T cell function
B: Grossly reduced levels of B cells
C: Defective isotype switching
D: Selective IgA deficiency

title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
content: Single gene primary immunodeficiencies like X-linked hyper IgM syndrome could be easily differentiated from selective IgA deficiency (For hyper-IgM syndrome, see 'History and Physical'). Low levels of IgA can be seen with deficiencies of one or more IgG subclasses. A normal response is seen to some vaccinations. Infections by encapsulated bacteria ( Hemophilus influenza and Streptococcus pneumoniae ) are seen here in recurrent sinopulmonary infections as they are in IgA deficiency.

title: Immunodeficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
content: Common variable immunodeficiency is a cause of recurrent bacterial infections or more rarely viral infections, but it is ruled out because the infections start later in life and mostly after childhood. All causes of antibody deficiency most be rule out before considering the diagnosis of this problem.

title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin A -- Clinical Significance
content: Selective IgA deficiency is the most prevalent immunodeficiency in humans and affects 1 in 700 individuals, most of the White race. It characteristically demonstrates an isolated decrease in IgA levels below 7 mg/dl with generally normal levels of IgG and Ig M in patients over four years of age. [7] It is due to an inability of IgA bearing B lymphocytes to switch into IgA secreting plasma cells. While around half of individuals are asymptomatic or have mild symptoms, those with more severe IgA deficiency have repeated bouts of bacterial, enteroviral, or protozoal infections of the gastrointestinal and respiratory tracts due to the lack of mucosal immunity. The most prevalent illnesses associated with IgA deficiency are recurrent sino-pulmonary infections. [7]

title: Immunodeficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
content: IgG selective deficiencies predispose to bacterial recurrent infections but they can be ruled out by the demonstration of absence or low serum levels of one or more IgG subclasses. This problem is corrected by the administration of gammaglobulins or intravenous immunoglobulins.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
content: Gastritis and duodenitis is a gastrointestinal inflammatory condition that is classified as a specific manifestation within the broader category of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). It can give rise to or be related to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, indicating a more severe or erosive form of gastric inflammation. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis has been associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى, suggesting the presence of bloody stool and possible lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي, indicating that patients who are HIV‑positive may be at increased risk for or present with this condition. Recognizing these relationships assists clinicians in evaluating disease severity, considering underlying immunocompromise, and selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
